coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanoswitch based on redox-controlled reversible sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of ochratoxin A
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Yuanjin
Qiu, Bin
Lin, Zhenyu
Guo, Longhua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots (SH-CDs) were synthesized for the first time by a two-step hydrothermal method. The sulfhydryl group in SH-CDs can be converted efficiently and reversibly in a few minutes under redox conditions. On this basis, ochratoxin A (OTA) induced hybrid chain reaction amplification and alkaline phosphatase enzymatic hydrolysis to produce ascorbic acid (AA). AA reduced the aggregated SH-CDs, thereby realizing the dual-mode rapid quantitative analysis of the OTA concentration by measuring the changes in the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the SH-CDs solution. The proposed sensor has a good linear relationship with the OTA concentration in the range of 2.0–5.0 nM, and the lowest detection limit is 2.0 nM. Additionally, the visual color change of the solution can improve the simplicity and accuracy of semi-quantitative detection of OTA with the naked eye. In short, this method has the advantages of rapidity, sensitivity, reversibility and visualization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Sulfhydryl group
Redox
Reversible
Ochratoxin A
Hybrid chain reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Plot the standard curve of OTA
Efficient and reversible conversion of SH-CDs under redox conditions
The sensing performance of H2O2, AA and ALP based on SH-CDs
Feasibility analysis of OTA triggering HCR
Plot the standard curve of OTA
The selectivity of the proposed sensing strategy
Detection of OTA content in red wine samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22074054 ), the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province ( 2020C02022 ), the Zhejiang Provincial High-level Talent Special Support Plan ( 2021R52044 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LQ20B050002 , LQ20B050004 , LGF22B050005 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2020J01305 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the principle of a fluorescent nanoswitch based on redox-controlled reversible SH-CDs for high-sensitivity detection of OTA.

Characterization of SH-CDs. (A) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, the inset shows the color of SH-CDs under visible light (left) and 365 nm excitation (right); (C) UV-Vis absorption spectra; (D) Transient fluorescence spectra; (E) FT-IR spectra; (F) TEM image, the inset shows the size distribution; (G) XPS spectra; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (H) and S 2p (I).

Feasibility analysis of SH-CDs responding to H2O2 and AA. (A) Fluorescence spectra, the inset shows the fluorescence color under 365 nm excitation; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra, the inset shows the solution color change under visible light; (C) TEM image of aggregated SH-CDs in the HRP-H2O2 system; (D) TEM image of dispersed SH-CDs after reacting with AA; (E) Kinetic curve of absorbance value with reaction time; (F) Cyclic reversible stability measurement.

Feasibility of HCR amplification and detection of OTA. (A) Schematic diagram; (B) PAGE analysis of DNA hybridization products; Absorbance value of the solution with or without HCR amplification (C) and target OTA (D).

Reversible aggregate SH-CDs are used to evaluate the analytical performance of detecting OTA. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of SH-CDs solutions after responding to different concentrations of OTA, the inset shows the color change of the corresponding SH-CDs solution; Absorbance value change curve (B) and standard curves (C) with OTA concentration; (D) Fluorescence spectra of SH-CDs solutions after responding to different concentrations of OTA; Fluorescence intensity change curve (E) and standard curves (F) with OTA concentration.
The proposed sensing strategy was used for the determination of OTA content in wine (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanoswitch based on redox-controlled reversible sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of ochratoxin A

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiaxing Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Sensing, College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Jiaxing University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots (SH-CDs) were synthesized for the first time by a two-step hydrothermal method. The sulfhydryl group in SH-CDs can be converted efficiently and reversibly in a few minutes under redox conditions. On this basis, ochratoxin A (OTA) induced hybrid chain reaction amplification and alkaline phosphatase enzymatic hydrolysis to produce ascorbic acid (AA). AA reduced the aggregated SH-CDs, thereby realizing the dual-mode rapid quantitative analysis of the OTA concentration by measuring the changes in the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the SH-CDs solution. The proposed sensor has a good linear relationship with the OTA concentration in the range of 2.0–5.0 nM, and the lowest detection limit is 2.0 nM. Additionally, the visual color change of the solution can improve the simplicity and accuracy of semi-quantitative detection of OTA with the naked eye. In short, this method has the advantages of rapidity, sensitivity, reversibility and visualization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm, consisting of sp2/sp3 carbon skeletons and abundant functional groups/polymer chains. [1] Carbon dots have the advantages of high luminescence efficiency, tunable luminescence, good photostability [2,3], low cost [4], excellent solubility [5], low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [6], and are therefore widely used in sensing detection [7–10], bioimaging [11], tumor therapy [12], and drug delivery [13].

It is well known that the currently reported sensing strategies based on carbon dots can detect almost only one analyte, and the carbon dots do not have reversible and recyclable properties [14–16], which greatly limits the universal application of carbon dots. Therefore, it is essential to design a reversible carbon dot for stimulus response [17–19]. The reversible transformation of carbon dots is influenced by factors such as redox, aggregation/dispersion, conformation and polarity. Among them, the disulfide bond (-S-S-) is formed by two sulfhydryl molecules under oxidizing conditions. On the contrary, reducing conditions [20–22] can effectively cleave them. Therefore, disulfide bonds can be used for the detection of redox substances [23] and controlled release of drugs [24].

Ochratoxin A (OTA) is a highly toxic and widely distributed fungal toxin [25] that can contaminate crops, fruits, and wine [26]. The consumption of OTA-contaminated food by humans and animals leads to the accumulation of OTA in the body, resulting in slower metabolism. Moreover, OTA is carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic, and is positioned as a 2B carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer [27]. The European Commission has established maximum permissible levels of OTA in different foods and related raw materials [28]. Therefore, the design and preparation of functional carbon dots and the construction of novel strategies for real-time monitoring and detection of OTA levels in foods are of great importance for the protection of people's health.

Herein, sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots (SH-CDs) were synthesized for the first time by a two-step hydrothermal method. The sulfhydryl groups (-SH) can be efficiently and reversibly converted under redox conditions, causing changes in the fluorescence intensity and absorbance values of SH-CDs. Combined with nucleic acid isothermal amplification technique and enzymatic reaction, the dual-mode quantitative detection of OTA content was achieved. The detailed principle is shown in Fig. 1, firstly, the OTA aptamer (OTA Apt) and its complementary chain cOTA (MB-cOTA) are modified on magnetic beads. In the absence of OTA, the hybridization chain reaction (HCR) and the subsequent series of reactions cannot be triggered. When OTA is present, HCR amplification is first triggered, generating a large number of biotin DNA duplexes. The duplex is then enriched with ALP through stronger affinity binding between biotin and streptavidin-modified alkaline phosphatase (SA-ALP). ALP hydrolyzes sodium L-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce ascorbic acid (AA) which was added to the aggregated SH-CDs solution, and the disulfide bonds in SH-CDs are broken reductively. The changes in fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the SH-CDs solution were measured to achieve dual-mode quantification of the OTA concentration. The method has the advantages of being rapid, reversible, sensitive and intuitive. Changing the nucleobase sequence of the target aptamer can achieve universal detection of food contaminants, tumor markers, environmental toxins and biological proteins, and its application can be further expanded by combining with other assays.

Citric acid (0.42 g) was added to the deionized water (10 mL) and dissolved it completely by ultrasound, then transferred the solution into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor and reacted at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm to obtain the supernatant, which is the carboxylic acid carbon dots. 4-Aminothiophenol (0.0313 g) was added to the carboxyl carbon dots (10 mL) and dissolved it completely by ultrasound, then the solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor and the reaction was continued at 135 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to obtain the supernatant, and further dialyzed with a dialysis bag (500 Da) for 24 h to obtain SH-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plot the standard curve of OTA
ce:section.ce:para: OTA Apt (1.0 μM, 15 μL) and different concentrations (0–6.0 nM) of OTA were sequentially added to NE buffer solution (pH=7.4, 1x) and reacted at 37 ℃ for 1 h. Then added MB-cOTA (10 μL) and continue to react on the rotary mixer at 37 ℃ for 1 h, and separated and washed five times with a magnetic separator. Then added 180 μL NE buffer solution (1x), Biotin-HP1 (10 μM, 10 μL) and bioton-HP2 (10 μM, 10 μL) in sequence and trigger the HCR amplification reaction at 37 ℃ to produce a large amount of biotin-containing DNA double-stranded [29]. The solution was separated and washed five times with a magnetic separator, added 180 μL PBS buffer solution (pH=7.4) and 20 μL SA-ALP (the original solution diluted 100 times) and bound through the specific affinity between biotin-avidin at 37 ℃, enriched the double strand with a lot of ALP. Separated and washed five times with a magnetic separator, added a Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=9.5, 50 mM) with a final concentration of 1.7 mM AAP and enzymatically hydrolyze at 37 ℃ to produce AA. The mixed solution after the reaction was added to the HRP-H2O2/SH-CDs system, vortexed for 30 s, and the fluorescence intensity and absorbance value of SH-CDs were measured and plotted as a standard curve with OTA concentration.

The emission wavelength of SH-CDs is almost independence of excitation, with the optimal emission wavelength located at 509 nm ( Fig. 2A) and the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength at 363 nm (Fig. 2B). Besides, the UV-Vis characteristic absorption peak of SH-CDs was close to the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2C), indicating that the fluorescence of SH-CDs originated from the n-π * transition of heteroatomic unsaturated groups [30]. The fitted fluorescence lifetime of SH-CDs was about 2.758 ns (Fig. 2D), and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield was 12.9%. FT-IR spectrum indicated that the characteristic peaks at 3200–3600, 1720, 1400 and 1196 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-OH/NH, CO, C-N and C-S, respectively. And SH- CDs showed the -SH stretching vibration peak at 2600 cm−1, indicating the existence of sulfhydryl functional groups in SH-CDs (Fig. 2E) [31]. TEM image showed that SH-CDs had good dispersibility, a regular spherical morphology, a narrow size distribution and an average diameter of 4.0 nm (Fig. 2F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) presented four characteristic peaks of C 1 s in SH-CDs at 288.91 eV, 286.75 eV, 285.14 eV and 284.44 eV, corresponding to CO, C-N/C-O, C-S and C-C, respectively (Fig. 2H). In addition, the high-resolution spectra of S 2p in SH-CDs have two characteristic peaks at 164.28 eV and 163.10 eV, corresponding to the 2p1/2 and 2p2/3 of C-S (Fig. 2I) [32]. Besides, the high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s and O 1 s in SH-CDs also illustrated their elemental composition and bonding type (Fig. S1). The above results revealed that the sulfur elements are mainly attached to the surface of SH-CDs as C-S in the form of -SH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient and reversible conversion of SH-CDs under redox conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The abundant -SH provide the possibility of redox regulation of the optical properties of SH-CDs. Fig. 3A presented the fluorescence of SH-CDs was significantly quenched under HRP-H2O2 oxidation conditions, which was mainly due to the strong oxidizing •OH produced in the HRP-H2O2 system, and •OH oxidized the -SH to form -S-S- bonds, inducing cross-link aggregation and fluorescence quenching between SH-CDs. Meanwhile, oxidation causes the color of SH-CDs solution to change from colorless to brownish-red (Fig. 3B), due to the significant increase in the size of the nanoparticles by cross-linking and aggregation (Fig. 3C) [33]. After adding AA to the above system, the fluorescence recovered and the color of the solution changed from brownish red to colorless. This was because the strong reducibility of AA led to the rupture of disulfide bonds [23], SH-CDs changed from the aggregate state to the dispersed state, and the nanoparticle size became smaller (Fig. 3D). Therefore, the changes based on fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectra indicated that SH-CDs can be reversibly transformed under redox conditions [34].

The kinetic curves of the absorbance values of SH-CDs solutions under redox conditions with reaction time were then investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 3E, the color development of the solution under oxidizing conditions was rapidly completed within 4 min. On the contrary, the color of the solution changed to colorless immediately (<5 s) after the addition of AA with a slight shaking. The above results indicated that SH-CDs were very efficient in responding to redox substances and can be completed within a few minutes, which was significantly faster than most reported sensors [35–38]. More importantly, visual qualitative analysis can be achieved by observing the color change of the solution with the naked eye. Moreover, the stability of SH-CDs under strong redox conditions was evaluated. Fig. 3F revealed that the absorbance value of SH-CDs was still close after 9 cycles under redox conditions with almost no decrease. The results demonstrated that SH-CD has excellent stability, can be used repeatedly and reversibly, and saves costs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing performance of H2O2, AA and ALP based on SH-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from the efficient redox reversible conversion of SH-CDs, a dual-mode sensor based on SH-CDs was constructed for the detection of various analytes. As shown in Fig. S2A, as the concentration of H2O2 gradually increased, the absorbance value of the solution gradually increased (Fig. S2B) and showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–750 μM, with a linear coefficient of 0.999 (Fig. S2C). When adding different concentrations of AA to the above mixed solution, the absorbance value of the solution gradually decreased (Fig. S2D) and showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–200 μM and 300–750 μM (Fig. S2E). The linear coefficients were 0.981 and 0.993, respectively (Fig. S2F).

Based on the above-mentioned excellent detection performance, SH-CDs nanoswitches were further used for ALP activity analysis. ALP hydrolyzed sodium ascorbyl phosphate (AAP) to produce AA, and the AA reduced the oxidation state of SH-CDs, causing SH-CDs to change from aggregated state to dispersed state. As given in Fig. S3A, as the ALP concentration gradually increased, the absorbance value of the solution gradually decreased (Fig. S3B) and showed a good linear relationship in the range of 200–550 U/L, with a linear coefficient of 0.993 (Fig. S3C). At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of the solution gradually increased, showing a good linear relationship in the range of 200–550 U/L, with a linear coefficient of 0.991 (Fig. S3F). The above results indicated that SH-CDs can be applied for the detection of redox substances and their extended applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility analysis of OTA triggering HCR
ce:section.ce:para: Combining SH-CDs with HCR for analyte versatility assays. Using OTA as a proof of concept, HCR and subsequent enzymatic reactions can be triggered only when OTA is present ( Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. 4B, when only cOTA, OTA Apt, HP1 and HP2 were present, short and bright bands can be observed (lanes a-d). After hybridization of cOTA and OTA Apt, cOTA and HP1, cOTA and OTA Apt/HP1, HP1 and HP2, a new band was observed (lane e-h). At the same time, the hybridization of cOTA with HP1/HP2 produced a new band (lane i) with a very large molecular weight and could not be separated, indicating that the HCR amplification reaction can be triggered. The hybridization of MB-cOTA with bio-HP1/HP2 triggered HCR amplification to produce a large amount of biotin-containing DNA double-strands, which can be combined with SA-ALP through the high affinity between biotin and avidin, making the double-strand rich ALP. ALP hydrolyzed AAP to produce AA. When AA was added to the aggregated SH-CDs solution, the disulfide bond was reduced and broken, returning from the aggregated state to the dispersed state, and the absorbance of the solution decreased significantly (Fig. 4C). Besides, in the absence of the target OTA, OTA Apt hybridized with MB-cOTA, and there was no free MB-cOTA in the solution, which cannot trigger HCR amplification, and the color of the solution remained brown-red, and the obvious absorbance value (red line) can be detected. When excess OTA was added, OTA and OTA Apt were completely combined, and there was no free OTA Apt in the solution, which cannot hybridize with MB-cOTA. At this time, the hybridization of MB-cOTA and bio-HP1/HP2 triggered HCR amplification, and the color of the solution changed from brownish red to colorless, and only a weak absorbance value (black line) can be detected (Fig. 4D). The above results proved the feasibility of this scheme and can be used for quantitative detection of OTA.

To achieve the best sensing performance in the shortest time and the lowest cost, it is essential to optimize the detection parameters. In this work, the concentration of MB-cOTA was fixed, and whether it can trigger HCR amplification was determined by the concentration of free OTA Apt. Therefore, the effect of OTA Apt concentration on HCR amplification was investigated. It can be seen from Fig. S4A, when the concentration of OTA Apt was in the range of 0–60 nM, OTA Apt hybridized with MB-cOTA completely and free MB-cOTA still existed in the solution, which further triggered HCR amplification and caused the disulfide bond breakage in SH-CDs, so that the absorbance value of the solution was significantly reduced. When the concentration of OTA Apt was greater than 60 nM and gradually increased, the free MB-cOTA concentration in the mixed solution gradually decreased, causing the HCR amplification product to gradually decrease, the disulfide bond reduction cleavage gradually decreased, and the absorbance value of the solution gradually increased. When the concentration of OTA Apt was 75 nM, MB-cOTA in the solution completely hybridized and cannot trigger HCR amplification, and the absorbance value of the solution was consistent with the aggregate state. To save the amount of OTA Apt, 75 nM was selected as the optimal OTA Apt concentration for subsequent experiments. Besides, the HCR amplification time determined the concentration of the HCR amplified product and ultimately determined the sensitivity of the target OTA. As shown in Fig. S4B, as the amplification time increased, the amplification products gradually increased, and the absorbance of the solution gradually decreased and reached a minimum at 55 min. Therefore, the HCR amplification time was selected as 55 min for subsequent detection. Ensuring that sufficient AA is produced by participating in the hydrolysis of ALP is essential for the detection of OTA, and the concentration of the substrate AAP is very critical. As shown in Fig. S4C, under the same amplification concentration and conditions, as the concentration of AAP gradually increased, the concentration of AA produced in the solution gradually increased, and the absorbance value of the solution gradually decreased. When the AAP concentration was 1.7 mM, the absorbance value was the lowest. At this time, the aggregated SH-CDs were completely reduced, so 1.7 mM was selected as the optimal substrate concentration. The time for ALP to hydrolyze AAP was further optimized. As shown in Fig. S4D, when the hydrolysis time was 35 min, the solution completely faded, indicating that the AA produced at this time was sufficient to reduce the disulfide bond in SH-CDs. Therefore, the optimal time for ALP to hydrolyze AAP is 35 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plot the standard curve of OTA
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, the target triggered HCR amplification and induced reduced aggregate SH-CDs for OTA detection. As manifested in Fig. 5 A, as the OTA concentration gradually increased, the solution gradually faded, the absorbance value gradually decreased, and the absorbance value had a good linear relationship with the OTA concentration in the range of 2.0–5.0 nM, and its correlation coefficient R2 was 0.998 (Fig. 5 C). SH-CDs were reduced from the aggregate state to the dispersed state, the fluorescence intensity gradually recovered, and the fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with the OTA concentration in the range of 2.0–5.0 nM, and its correlation coefficient R2 was 0.995 (Fig. 5D-F), and the minimum detection limit was 2.0 nM (0.808 ng/mL), which met the detection requirements of the national food OTA content limit standard (<2–10 ng/mL). Compared with the previously reported method for determining OTA (Table S2), the proposed strategy exhibited comparable or higher sensitivity, indicating that redox-controlled reversible SH-CDs can be used as nano-switches for highly sensitive quantitative analysis of OTA. More importantly, the color change of the solution induced by OTA can be distinguished with the naked eye, easy to implement, and the determination of OTA in food can be achieved without expensive equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the proposed sensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Excellent selectivity is a prerequisite for specific detection of the target, which is of great significance in actual detection. To investigate the specificity of the proposed biosensor for OTA, OTB, AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2, DON, ZEN and MC were selected as interfering substances and reacted under the same conditions. As displayed in Fig. S5, when the target OTA was present, the absorbance value of the solution is significantly decreased, and when the concentration of the interfering substance is 50 times the OTA concentration (6.0 nM), the absorbance value of the solution was basically close to that of the aggregated SH-CDs solution. The results indicated that these interfering substances do not cause HCR amplification and enzymatic reactions, and cannot reduce aggregated SH-CDs, indicating that the sensor has high selectivity for OTA. In addition, five parallel determinations were performed in the presence of OTA (6.0 nM), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.24%, indicating that the proposed redox reversible nanoswitch biosensor has good repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTA content in red wine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of the biosensor in detecting actual samples, the OTA in the red wine of the two brands (Great Wall Dry Red and SEN Burg Chateau) was extracted and spiked in accordance with the national standard (GB 5009.96–2016). The d.

etermination results were listed in Table 1. The recovery rate was in the range of 104.25–109%, and the RSD was 3.6–5.2%. It showed that the proposed method can be used for the quantitative determination of OTA in food, and had certain feasibility and application prospects.

In conclusion, functionalized SH-CDs can be efficiently and reversibly converted under redox conditions, which induces dual-mode changes in the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of SH-CDs. On the basis, target-triggered HCR and enzymatic reactions were combined with SH-CDs for the dual-mode determination of OTA and successfully applied to the detection of OTA content in actual red wine samples. Only changing the DNA sequence of the aptamer of the target can realize the universal detection of food contaminants, tumor markers, environmental toxins and biological proteins and other molecules. However, the strategy still has some shortcomings that need to be improved. First, although the proposed detection strategy has excellent selectivity and sensitivity, the whole detection process is time-consuming. Secondly, the strategy still relies on large instruments, and it will be a major challenge to use solution color change combined with miniature smart devices to build POCT technology for rapid reading, quantitative analysis and in-situ detection of OTA content.
Yuanjin Zhan: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Bin Qiu: Supervision. Zhenyu Lin: Supervision. Longhua Guo: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074054), the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province (2020C02022), the Zhejiang Provincial High-level Talent Special Support Plan (2021R52044), and the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20B050002, LQ20B050004, LGF22B050005), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020J01305).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133693.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuanjin Zhan is currently a postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Chemistry at Fudan University. His research interests focus on the preparation and application of quantum dots, biological applications of two-dimensional nanomaterials.

Bin Qiu is currently a professor in the College of Chemistry at Fuzhou University (China). He obtained his PhD in analytical chemistry from Fuzhou University in 2008. Subsequently, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Hong Kong Baptist University. His research focuses on biosensors for food safety analysis.

Zhenyu Lin is currently a professor in the College of Chemistry at Fuzhou University (China). He obtained his PhD in analytical chemistry from Fuzhou University. Subsequently, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Graduate School of Environmental Studies & School of Engineering, Tohoku University (Japan). His research focuses on biosensors for food safety analysis and disease forewarning & diagnosis.

Longhua Guo is currently a full Professor in the College of Biological, Chemical Sciences and Engineering at Jiaxing University He obtained his Ph. D from Fuzhou University in 2009. He worked as a postdoctoral research fellow in School of Chemical and Biomedical engineering, Nanyang Technological University from 2009 to 2011. His research interests focus on the fabrication of nano-optical devices for environmental and biological applications.
